Tafseer:
The stubborn group among the People of the Book and the polytheists: Here, 'lam yakun' is not used as an auxiliary verb, but rather as a complete verb, just as in: (And Allah is ever Knowing and Wise) (An-Nisa, 4:17). Therefore, its translation should be that those who disbelieved in the Qur'an and the Messenger from among the People of the Book and the polytheists were not going to abandon their stubbornness until a clear proof came to them.
Based on context, the object of 'kafaru' (disbelieved) is omitted here—meaning: those who rejected the Qur'an and the Messenger.
'The People of the Book' and 'the polytheists' are mentioned as two groups who were foremost in opposing the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) during that era. The polytheists opposed him openly, while the People of the Book, as noted in the introduction, did so secretly. When the word 'mushrikeen' (polytheists) is used in this manner, it serves as a proper noun for the polytheists of the Quraysh or the Children of Ishmael. Several clear examples of this usage exist in the Qur'an.
The particle 'min' (from) is used here in its recognized partitive sense, because this describes the character of the specific group among the polytheists and the People of the Book who rose blindly to oppose Islam. Not everyone in these two groups was identical; rather, there were reasonable individuals among them who accepted Islam, or at least remained moderate or neutral. The Qur'an has praised such individuals. Here, 'min' is used to highlight this difference—showing that this describes only the character of the group that was unwilling to listen or understand anything without seeing their demanded miracles.
The demand of the stubborn: 'until there came to them the clear proof' (hatta ta'tiyahumu-l-bayyinah) - This describes the condition without which these stubborn individuals were not ready to abandon their obstinacy. 'Bayyinah' means a clear proof or sign. By 'clear proof', they meant a prominent miracle that left no room for denial. Their demand is mentioned in various places of the Qur'an. We present some examples to illustrate their mindset.
In Surah An-Nisa, the demand of the People of the Book is mentioned as:
(The People of the Book ask you to bring down to them a book from the heaven. But they had asked of Moses [even] greater than that and said, 'Show us Allah outright') (An-Nisa, 4:153).
Similarly, regarding the polytheists, the Qur'an states in Surah Al-Muddaththir:
(Rather, every person among them desires that he be given scriptures spread open) (Al-Muddaththir, 74:52).
This means they were not ready to believe in this Qur'an which was revealed through inspiration to a single person, but demanded that distinct, open scriptures descend from heaven for every individual among them, before they would believe that Allah had indeed revealed a book for their guidance.

This explains 'the clear proof' (Al-Bayyinah)—showing that their demand is: 'If we are to believe, the way to do so is for an angel to descend directly from Allah, reciting pure, pristine pages containing clear and definitive commands.'
The words 'a Messenger from Allah' (rasulum-minallah) show that they did not desire a human messenger who claimed to receive revelation; rather, they demanded an angel coming directly from Allah, reciting pure pages.
'Pure pages' (suhufam-mutahharah) - 'Sahifah' also means a page. The meaning is that they would believe the angel only if he did not merely speak, but brought written pages from God and recited them. The attribute 'mutahharah' (purified/pure) implies that these pages should be pristine, untouched by any jinn or human besides God and the angel.

'Within them are correct writings' (feeha kutubun qayyimah) - In addition to the aforementioned conditions, they also demanded that these pages should not contain various historical narratives or stories, but straightforward, definitive, and clear commands, so they could easily know what their Lord commands and what He forbids.
'Kutub' is the plural of 'kitab' (book). As explained under Surah Al-Baqarah, (Footnote 1) this word is used in the Qur'an in the sense of the Shariah's commands, and here it is used in this very sense.
'Qayyimah' means straight, clear, and definitive. That is: 'We should be given definitive commands; we do not wish to hear unrelated matters.' The subtle sarcasm directed at the Qur'an by these wicked critics through this word is obvious. They meant: just as the Ten Commandments of the Torah were given to Moses on tablets, similar clear commands written on pages should be brought down by an angel from heaven before they would accept it as a book from God.
_____
Footnote 1: Refer to Tadabbur-i-Qur'an, Vol. 1, p. 42.

Answering the demand initiated by the People of the Book: Since this demand was formulated by the People of the Book, who taught it to the leaders of the Quraysh to use against the Qur'an, the Qur'an addressed them directly in its response. It is stated: even if a miracle was shown to them in accordance with their demand, they would still not believe. Their history bears witness that the Shariah was given to them amidst prominent miracles, and their prophet, Moses (peace be upon him), showed them wonders of divine power at every step that had never been shown to any nation before. The Ten Commandments of the Torah were written and given on tablets. While taking the covenant, the mountain was suspended over them like a canopy, shade and manna and quails were arranged for them in the desert, and twelve springs were opened from a rock. Yet, they fulfilled the right of loyalty to the Shariah by making a calf under Samiri's direction and worshipping it as their deity. This very corruption initiated the divisions among them, which grew with time until nothing remained shared among them except disagreement.

This is an example of the division mentioned in the preceding verse. It is stated: not a single thing among the fundamental teachings of the religion remains upon which they stand firm; rather, they have split in every path, losing the religion through their disagreements.
They were commanded to observe monotheism—worshipping Allah alone, devoting their obedience sincerely to Him, and remaining upright—but they destroyed this fundamental teaching. During their prophet's presence, they worshipped a calf, made Ezra the son of God, and made their rabbis and priests 'lords besides Allah'. They adopted magic and sorcery, and even worshipped the idols of other nations, which their prophets lamented with great sorrow.
Similarly, they were commanded with prayer and zakat, but they completely neglected prayer, as explained under Surah Al-Baqarah, to the extent that its mention is virtually lost in the Torah. While sacrifice is mentioned in the Torah, prayer is almost non-existent. The same happened to zakat. While it remained symbolically, its true recipients—the poor and needy—were replaced by the Levite priests and rabbis, whose greed and miserliness can be seen in the portrayals documented in the Gospels and other prophetic scriptures.
'And that is the correct religion' (wa dhalika deenu-l-qayyimah) - This answers their demand mentioned in: 'Within them are correct writings'. The meaning is: if they truly seek a straight and clear religion, then these commands were given as the teachings of the straight, natural religion of the Abrahamic faith—so why did they destroy them? And since this Qur'an is calling them to these same clear and definitive teachings, why are they opposing it? The point is: the questions raised by the People of the Book are merely the product of their envy. They wish that just as they have deprived themselves of the Shariah, others should remain deprived as well.
The correct religion: 'deenu-l-qayyimah' is originally 'deenu-l-millati-l-qayyimah' (the religion of the correct community). The noun 'al-millah' is omitted here, which is grammatically accepted. This points to the reality that these fundamental teachings were inherited by both branches of Abraham's family. It is a pity if both branches collude to oppose these very teachings.
Note that the Abrahamic community is described in the Qur'an as 'the correct community' (millatan qayyimah). We discussed this in its relevant place; here, we cite a single verse:
(Say, 'Indeed, my Lord has guided me to a straight path—a correct religion, the community of Abraham, inclining toward truth.') (Al-An'am, 6:161).

Dealing a blow to the pride of the People of the Book and the polytheists: This describes the end of those who persist in denying the Qur'an, whether from the People of the Book or the polytheists, and deals a blow to the pride that caused their denial.
It is stated: whoever disbelieves by denying the Qur'an will be cast into the Fire of Hell, whether from the People of the Book or the polytheists. Having shown the same blindness as the polytheists, the People of the Book deserve no leniency. Furthermore, it is emphasized that they will abide in Hell eternally, and will not be released after a few days. This refutes their delusion: (The Fire will not touch us except for a few days).
'They are the worst of creatures' (ula'ika hum sharru-l-bariyyah) - This deals a blow to their pride. The condition they placed—that they would not believe in the Prophethood of Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) unless an angel descended with open scriptures, or distinct pages were given to each of them—was based on the pride: how could they accept the authority of an ordinary person among them who was inferior to them in wealth? Their pride prevented them from accepting the truth, whereas submitting to the truth is obligatory on everyone, whether king or slave. If anyone displays pride against the truth, he is of the progeny of Satan, whose only destination is Hell.

The description of those who remained free of pride: This describes those servants of Allah who remained free of pride; therefore, respect for the truth remained in them. Hearing the Prophet's call, they did not make demands like the arrogant, but believed in His Book and chose the path of righteous deeds. It is stated: they are the best of creatures. This is because a person's value is determined by his intellect and moral qualities, not by his wealth or lineage. Those who possess these qualities are the true nobles in Allah's sight, even if they are Roman or Abyssinian slaves; and those who lack them are the worst of creatures, even if they are from the leaders of the Quraysh. As you read this, recall the taunts the leaders of the Quraysh cast upon the poor Muslims. Imagine how their heads must have been raised by this honor from the Lord of the heavens and earth!
Note that man is the most noble of creation, created in the best of molds with superior capabilities, as explained in Surah At-Tin. If he recognizes his value, he is 'the best of creatures' (khayru-l-bariyyah), with no equal in creation. But if he lives heedless of his value, he is 'the worst of creatures' (sharru-l-bariyyah) and the prime example of: (We return him to the lowest of the low). He falls into a depth of decline that is specific to him alone; no other creation falls to such depth. Just as there is no limit to his height, there is no limit to his decline. Wise is the saying: 'O man, know thyself!'

This describes their reward: they must face the trials destined for them in this world, but with their Lord, they will have gardens of eternity beneath which rivers flow, abiding therein forever. 'Gardens of eternity' (jannatu 'adn) was explained in its relevant place.
'Allah is pleased with them and they are pleased with Him' (radiya-llahu 'anhum wa radoo 'anh) - Allah is pleased with them because they fulfilled the right of worship in the manner He approved, and they are pleased with Allah because He not only fulfilled His promises, but blessed them with what they could never have imagined.
'That is for him who has feared his Lord' (dhalika liman khashiya rabbah) - This is for those who feared their Lord in the unseen. Those who insist on seeing everything before they believe will remain wandering. The primary test in this world is to use intellect to believe in the unseen realities reported by the messengers. A person should not live with closed eyes, waiting to believe only when everything is displayed before him. Whoever passes this test deserves His great favor; and whoever fails is worse than animals, and is unworthy of His favor.
